title: PureHealth launches University of Nicosia Athens Campus to drive digital medical education
summary: The new campus brings a modern approach to training healthcare professionals by embedding artificial intelligence, data analytics, and digital health tools directly into clinical learning.
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
GREECE—PureHealth has officially opened the University of Nicosia Athens campus in Greece via its Greek subsidiary, marking a major step in its drive to reshape medical education through technology and integrated care systems.

The new campus brings a modern approach to training healthcare professionals by embedding artificial intelligence, data analytics, and digital health tools directly into clinical learning.

High-level launch attended by regional leaders

The inauguration ceremony drew senior leaders from government, diplomacy, education, and healthcare across Greece, Cyprus, and the UAE, reflecting the regional importance of the initiative.

Among the dignitaries were the Prime Minister of Greece, Kyriakos Mitsotakis, and the President of Cyprus, Nikos Christodoulides, alongside education ministers Sofia Zacharaki and Athina Michaelidou.

In addition, UAE Ambassador to Greece Dr. Ali Obaid Ali Al Dhaheri, PureHealth Group CEO Shaista Asif, and senior representatives from the University of Nicosia also attended, reinforcing the project’s cross-border academic and healthcare collaboration.

A new model for tech-enabled medical training

The UNIC Athens campus introduces a forward-looking education model that blends academic learning with real-world clinical exposure.

By integrating AI-driven tools and digital health systems into the curriculum, the university aims to prepare medical graduates for increasingly data-centric healthcare environments.

PureHealth positioned the initiative as part of its broader strategy to align education, research, and clinical care within a single ecosystem.

Through its international network, the group plans to transfer expertise and operational standards across the UAE, Cyprus, and Greece, strengthening consistency in healthcare delivery.

Strengthening regional collaboration and workforce development

Speaking at the event, UAE representatives highlighted the initiative as a reflection of strong bilateral ties with Greece and a shared commitment to advancing healthcare innovation.

They noted that the project supports long-term investment in talent development and knowledge exchange across borders.

Greek education officials emphasized that the campus strengthens the country’s ambition to become a regional hub for higher education and innovation.

They also pointed to regulatory frameworks that support the entry of internationally recognized institutions into Greece’s academic landscape.

Addressing Europe’s healthcare workforce gap

The initiative comes at a time when Europe faces a growing shortage of healthcare professionals, projected to reach nearly one million workers by 2030.

At the same time, healthcare systems are rapidly adopting AI and digital technologies, increasing demand for tech-skilled medical professionals.

To respond to these pressures, the UNIC Athens model integrates “living lab” environments where students train in real clinical settings.

Learners will also gain access to PureHealth’s healthcare facilities in Greece, which collectively serve over 1.3 million patients annually.

Cross-border training and future mobility

Beyond Greece, the university will establish exchange programs linking students and healthcare professionals with institutions in the UAE.

These initiatives aim to strengthen mobility, support shared learning, and build a more connected healthcare workforce across key international markets.
